news_summary: Today’s environmental news highlights the power of indigenous knowledge and natural ecosystems, such as vast climate-resilient coral reefs and targeted methane reduction, as effective solutions in combating climate change.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant le pouvoir des savoirs autochtones et des écosystèmes naturels, tels que les vastes récifs coralliens résistants au changement climatique et la réduction ciblée des émissions de méthane, en tant que solutions efficaces pour lutter contre le changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan el poder del conocimiento indígena y de los ecosistemas naturales —como los extensos arrecifes de coral resistentes al cambio climático y la reducción selectiva de las emisiones de metano— como soluciones eficaces para combatir el cambio climático.

articles.title: Indigenous cultural practices are a climate solution, report finds
articles.content:
As the impacts of climate change continue to escalate, a growing number of climate scientists and policymakers cite Indigenous lands as a model for their rich biodiversity and effective carbon storage. But that recognition has not always translated into space for Indigenous leaders in climate negotiations, access to climate resilience funding, or enforcement of human rights standards.

That has been the case for decades. But the problems do not stop there. New research shows that approach overlooks the key role that Indigenous knowledge and culture can play in mitigating climate change. It also reveals a dangerous misconception that has taken hold in global climate discussions: the idea that Indigenous lands are so rich because they are remote or sparsely populated.

Nothing could be further from the truth. The health of Indigenous lands and their ability to store vast quantities of carbon stem from the stewardship of the people who inhabit them.

That’s the finding of research from Conservation International, which shows that traditional knowledge, community protocol, and Indigenous culture play a direct role in protecting forests, wildlife, and the environment. Sushma Shrestha, who is Indigenous Newar from Nepal and the study’s lead author, said the research comes at a critical time.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“All of humanity relies on everything that Indigenous peoples have to contribute and offer in terms of their lands, in terms of carbon storage, in terms of biodiversity conservation,” she said.

The study, released as a narrative report and a peer-reviewed study, explores how Indigenous knowledge and practices benefit the planet. It also found that all 43 of the surveyed communities are experiencing drought, extreme weather, and other adverse impacts from climate change. More than half are affected by extractive industries like mining and logging.

Researchers interviewed 49 Indigenous leaders from six continents about how they steward their land, which ranged from the Amazon rainforest to East African savannas and Pacific Islands. They found that traditional management practices like avoiding overfishing, maintaining sacred spaces, watching for fires and other threats, and direct resistance against extraction contribute to the remarkable health of Indigenous territory. Ninety-six percent of respondents said they had land set aside for special uses, like spiritual practices, that also benefit the environment by protecting those spaces and ecosystems. Shrestha stressed that although each Indigenous community is distinct, there are shared lessons the entire world can learn from.

“It’s a time where all hands need to be on deck,” Shrestha said. “And collectively, actions need to be taken, and indigenous peoples have been doing this on their own for a very long time.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The study builds on years of research that shows, among other things, that the world’s healthiest forests are on Indigenous lands and conservation efforts are more effective when they incorporate Indigenous autonomy and decision-making at every step.

Drought and extreme weather were the top climate impacts cited, but 61 percent of those interviewed also called mining, commercial agriculture, logging, and other incursions serious concerns. These issues threaten to disrupt land stewardship practices that have endured for millennia. To help mitigate these challenges, Indigenous peoples are asking for mitigation and resilience funding, legal advice for protecting their territories, and recognition of national and international land rights.

“Indigenous Peoples’ Knowledge cannot exist without Indigenous Peoples or without the ecosystems where we live,” Hindou Oumarou Ibrahim, who is Indigenous Mbororo from Chad and a former chair of the United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, wrote in a forward to the report. “To protect our knowledge, there is an urgent need to recognize us, and our rights and lands must be secured.”

Respondents from a wide range of countries, including Bolivia, Mexico, and the Philippines, mentioned using community monitoring or patrols to protect their land from outsiders and violations of traditional protocol. Several also called for stronger legal protections to protect their lands from being sold or developed.

Shrestha, Ibrahim, and other Indigenous experts said that as much as Indigenous peoples are asking for help, they are also urging the world to learn from them. The Kichwa people in Ecuador, for example, restrict hunting of female tapirs and other animals to help slow population decline. The Tacana people in Bolivia, among others, do not permit tree clearing along rivers, which helps maintain water quality and prevent erosion. The list goes on, and could help the rest of the world mitigate climate change and protect the environment.

“It is my hope the voices of the sisters and brothers from all over the world reflected in this report trigger the action we need for the planet we all want, the action we need for Indigenous Peoples Knowledge to flourish, and honor our grandparents and our children that are yet to come,” Ibrahim said.

Given the scale of the threats facing the world and the urgency with which they must be addressed, Shrestha said that policy changes and enforcement of Indigenous land rights is more important than ever. She also stressed that these actions will benefit the entire world. “One thing that everybody can do, whether that is at the national level, or at the global level, is to really secure indigenous peoples’ rights to lands,” she said.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and positive real-world impact by showing how Indigenous cultural practices and knowledge contribute directly to climate change mitigation, biodiversity conservation, and carbon storage. It emphasizes the broad significance of Indigenous stewardship for global environmental health and calls for policy changes to secure Indigenous land rights, which benefits the entire planet. The article provides substantial context and detailed examples from diverse Indigenous communities worldwide, making it a focused and meaningful story about environmental conservation and climate action.
articles.personality_title: Indigenous cultural knowledge helps protect forests and fight climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Climate change is causing serious problems like drought and extreme weather around the world. Scientists have noticed that Indigenous lands often have healthy forests and store a lot of carbon, which helps slow climate change. But Indigenous leaders have not always been included in climate talks or given enough support.

**What happened** – A new study by Conservation International looked at 49 Indigenous leaders from six continents. It found that their traditional ways of caring for the land—like protecting sacred places, avoiding overfishing, and watching for fires—help keep forests and wildlife healthy. These practices are not just old customs but active ways to protect the environment.

**Impact** – The study shows that Indigenous culture and knowledge are important tools in fighting climate change. This is different from the wrong idea that Indigenous lands are healthy just because they are remote or empty. Instead, their care and rules keep the land rich and full of life. The research also found that many Indigenous communities face threats from mining, logging, and farming, which can harm their land and traditions.

**What's next step** – Indigenous people want stronger legal protections for their land and more funding to help them deal with climate change. Experts say governments should support these rights because protecting Indigenous land helps the whole planet. The study encourages the world to learn from Indigenous ways to better protect nature.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Indigenous peoples’ traditional knowledge and care of their lands play a crucial role in protecting forests and fighting climate change worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le savoir culturel autochtone protège les forêts et lutte contre le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le changement climatique provoque des problèmes graves comme la sécheresse et les phénomènes météorologiques extrêmes dans le monde. Les scientifiques ont remarqué que les terres autochtones ont souvent des forêts en bonne santé et stockent beaucoup de carbone, ce qui aide à ralentir le changement climatique. Mais les chefs autochtones ne sont pas toujours inclus dans les discussions climatiques ni assez soutenus.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude de Conservation International a interrogé 49 chefs autochtones de six continents. Elle a révélé que leurs méthodes traditionnelles de gestion de la terre—comme protéger les lieux sacrés, éviter la surpêche et surveiller les incendies—aident à garder les forêts et la faune en bonne santé. Ces pratiques ne sont pas seulement des coutumes anciennes, mais des moyens actifs de protéger l’environnement.

**Impact** – L’étude montre que la culture et le savoir autochtones sont des outils importants pour lutter contre le changement climatique. Contrairement à l’idée fausse que les terres autochtones sont saines parce qu’elles sont éloignées ou peu peuplées, c’est leur soin et leurs règles qui maintiennent ces terres riches et vivantes. L’étude note aussi que de nombreuses communautés autochtones sont menacées par l’exploitation minière, la déforestation et l’agriculture.

**Prochaine étape** – Les peuples autochtones demandent des protections légales renforcées pour leurs terres et plus de financements pour faire face au changement climatique. Les experts disent que les gouvernements doivent soutenir ces droits car protéger les terres autochtones profite à toute la planète. L’étude encourage le monde à apprendre de ces méthodes pour mieux protéger la nature.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le savoir et la gestion traditionnels des peuples autochtones jouent un rôle essentiel dans la protection des forêts et la lutte contre le changement climatique dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: El conocimiento cultural indígena protege bosques y combate el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cambio climático está causando problemas graves como sequías y fenómenos meteorológicos extremos en todo el mundo. Los científicos han notado que las tierras indígenas suelen tener bosques saludables y almacenan mucho carbono, lo que ayuda a frenar el cambio climático. Pero los líderes indígenas no siempre han sido incluidos en las conversaciones climáticas ni han recibido suficiente apoyo.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio de Conservation International entrevistó a 49 líderes indígenas de seis continentes. Encontró que sus formas tradicionales de cuidar la tierra—como proteger lugares sagrados, evitar la sobrepesca y vigilar incendios—ayudan a mantener saludables los bosques y la vida silvestre. Estas prácticas no son solo costumbres antiguas, sino formas activas de proteger el medio ambiente.

**Impacto** – El estudio muestra que la cultura y el conocimiento indígena son herramientas importantes para combatir el cambio climático. Esto es diferente a la idea equivocada de que las tierras indígenas son saludables solo porque están alejadas o poco pobladas. En cambio, su cuidado y reglas mantienen la tierra rica y llena de vida. También encontraron que muchas comunidades indígenas enfrentan amenazas por la minería, la tala y la agricultura.

**Próximo paso** – Los pueblos indígenas quieren protecciones legales más fuertes para sus tierras y más fondos para enfrentar el cambio climático. Los expertos dicen que los gobiernos deben apoyar estos derechos porque proteger las tierras indígenas ayuda a todo el planeta. El estudio anima al mundo a aprender de estas formas para proteger mejor la naturaleza.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El conocimiento y cuidado tradicional de los pueblos indígenas juegan un papel crucial en la protección de bosques y la lucha contra el cambio climático en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a lush, diverse landscape representing Indigenous stewardship: a rich forest with a flowing river bordered by sacred groves marked by subtle, glowing symbols of traditional community protection, alongside silhouettes of native animals like tapirs and fish thriving peacefully, all bathed in warm, earthy tones that convey harmony between culture, land, and climate resilience.

articles.title: Scientists find 64,000 sq miles of climate-resilient coral reef
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM For years, the conversation around coral reefs has been threaded with grief. Bleaching events, rising ocean temperatures, one crisis folding into the next. The reefs have become a kind of shorthand for what we stand to lose. A new global analysis is pushing back on that, and not with […]
The post Scientists find 64,000 sq miles of climate-resilient coral reef first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

For years, the conversation around coral reefs has been threaded with grief. Bleaching events, rising ocean temperatures, one crisis folding into the next. The reefs have become a kind of shorthand for what we stand to lose. A new global analysis is pushing back on that, and not with vague optimism, but with coordinates.

Researchers have identified approximately 64,000 square miles (166,000 square kilometers) of coral reefs capable of surviving and recovering from climate change, a figure three times larger than previous estimates. The analysis drew on 45,000 coral surveys and decades of climate and ocean data, finding resilient reef systems across 71 countries and 100 territories, including parts of the Caribbean and the Pacific and Atlantic oceans not previously known for their potential.

“Coral reefs are often framed as ecosystems beyond saving,” said Emily Darling, director of coral conservation at the Wildlife Conservation Society and one of the report’s authors. “This research shows otherwise: we know where the hope is, and what we need now is political will.”

Why the finding matters beyond the numbers

When the working assumption is that reef loss is inevitable, conservation energy tends to bleed away. There’s a logic to it: why fight for something you’ve already written off? But when you have a specific, defensible map of which reefs can make it through, that changes.

Stacy Jupiter, co-author of the study and executive director of the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Global Marine Program, puts it plainly: some reefs may need to be left behind so that others can be saved. For those that fall below certain benchmarks for ecosystem function, she said, the honest call is triage.

“In certain cases, where reefs are below certain benchmarks for ecosystem function, it may be a case of triage, where we may need to leave those places,” she said.

It’s a hard thing to say out loud. But spreading limited funds thinly across reefs that won’t recover doesn’t help the ones that will.

Only 28 percent of resilient reefs are currently protected

Of the newly identified climate-resilient reefs, only 28 percent fall within formally protected or conserved areas. The rest sit outside any official framework, and that’s where the research gets urgent.

Countries around the world are drafting action plans under the “30 by 30” target, a global commitment to bring 30 percent of land and marine environments under formal protection by 2030. This research gives governments a scientifically grounded starting point for deciding which marine areas to include.

“Only 28 percent of the reefs currently fall within protected and conserved areas, so the opportunity is clear, and so is the urgency, especially as we face an upcoming super El Niño event,” Darling said.

How this research could reshape marine conservation

A super El Niño event, which brings an intensified warming of Pacific Ocean temperatures, is expected in the coming years, and mass bleaching tends to follow. The reefs in this study are those most likely to survive those conditions and bounce back. Protecting them before the next warming cycle hits is the window currently open.

Jupiter described the data as a tool governments can use to direct limited conservation funds where they’ll do the most. The research covers regions that hadn’t previously made it onto conservation priority lists, giving countries drawing up marine protection plans somewhere concrete to start. The dominant story about coral reefs has been one of loss. This research is a different kind of story, and it comes with a map.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of 64,000 square miles of climate-resilient coral reefs, which is three times larger than previous estimates. This finding has broad environmental significance as it identifies specific reefs capable of surviving climate change, providing a clear, actionable path for conservation efforts globally. The research offers a hopeful, data-driven foundation for governments to prioritize marine protection, aligning with global targets like '30 by 30' and addressing urgent climate threats such as super El Niño events. The article is focused, detailed, and highlights a positive shift in coral reef conservation strategy with potential large-scale environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map 64,000 square miles of coral reefs that can survive climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Coral reefs around the world have been in danger for years because of warming oceans and bleaching. Many people believed that losing these reefs was unavoidable.

**What happened** – Scientists studied 45,000 coral surveys and climate data to find about 64,000 square miles (166,000 square kilometers) of coral reefs that are strong enough to survive and recover from climate change. This area is three times larger than what was thought before. These reefs are spread across 71 countries and 100 territories, including some new places in the Caribbean and the Pacific and Atlantic oceans.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows exactly where coral reefs can still be saved. It helps governments and conservation groups focus their efforts and money on reefs that have the best chance to survive. Right now, only 28% of these strong reefs are protected, so there is a big chance to improve conservation. Protecting these reefs is especially urgent because a strong El Niño event, which can cause more ocean warming and coral bleaching, is expected soon.

**What's next step** – Countries can use this new map to decide which reefs to protect as part of global goals like the “30 by 30” plan, which aims to protect 30% of land and ocean areas by 2030. Scientists warn that some weaker reefs may have to be left behind to focus resources on the healthier ones. This research gives a clear starting point for better marine conservation.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have identified a large, detailed map of coral reefs that can survive climate change, offering a clear path for targeted protection efforts worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques cartographient 64 000 milles carrés de récifs coralliens résistants au changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les récifs coralliens du monde entier sont en danger depuis des années à cause du réchauffement des océans et du blanchissement. Beaucoup pensaient que leur perte était inévitable.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié 45 000 relevés de coraux et des données climatiques pour découvrir environ 64 000 milles carrés (166 000 kilomètres carrés) de récifs coralliens capables de survivre et de se régénérer malgré le changement climatique. Cette surface est trois fois plus grande que ce que l’on croyait auparavant. Ces récifs se trouvent dans 71 pays et 100 territoires, y compris dans des zones nouvelles des Caraïbes et des océans Pacifique et Atlantique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre précisément où les récifs peuvent encore être sauvés. Elle permet aux gouvernements et aux groupes de conservation de concentrer leurs efforts et leurs fonds sur les récifs ayant les meilleures chances de survie. Actuellement, seulement 28 % de ces récifs résistants sont protégés, ce qui montre une grande opportunité d’amélioration. Protéger ces récifs est urgent, surtout avec l’arrivée prochaine d’un fort phénomène El Niño qui peut causer plus de réchauffement et de blanchissement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays peuvent utiliser cette nouvelle carte pour décider quels récifs protéger dans le cadre d’objectifs mondiaux comme le plan « 30 par 30 », qui vise à protéger 30 % des terres et océans d’ici 2030. Les scientifiques avertissent que certains récifs plus fragiles devront peut-être être abandonnés pour concentrer les ressources sur les récifs en meilleure santé. Cette recherche offre une base claire pour une meilleure conservation marine.

**En une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont identifié une grande carte détaillée des récifs coralliens capables de survivre au changement climatique, offrant une voie claire pour des efforts de protection ciblés dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean 64,000 millas cuadradas de arrecifes de coral resistentes al cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los arrecifes de coral en todo el mundo han estado en peligro durante años debido al calentamiento de los océanos y el blanqueamiento. Muchas personas creían que perder estos arrecifes era inevitable.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron 45,000 encuestas de coral y datos climáticos para encontrar aproximadamente 64,000 millas cuadradas (166,000 kilómetros cuadrados) de arrecifes que son lo suficientemente fuertes para sobrevivir y recuperarse del cambio climático. Esta área es tres veces más grande de lo que se pensaba antes. Estos arrecifes están repartidos en 71 países y 100 territorios, incluyendo nuevas zonas en el Caribe y los océanos Pacífico y Atlántico.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra exactamente dónde se pueden salvar los arrecifes. Ayuda a gobiernos y grupos de conservación a enfocar sus esfuerzos y dinero en los arrecifes con mejor oportunidad de sobrevivir. Actualmente, solo el 28 % de estos arrecifes resistentes están protegidos, por lo que hay una gran oportunidad para mejorar la conservación. Proteger estos arrecifes es urgente, especialmente porque se espera un fuerte evento de El Niño que puede causar más calentamiento y blanqueamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los países pueden usar este nuevo mapa para decidir qué arrecifes proteger como parte de metas globales como el plan “30 por 30”, que busca proteger el 30 % de las tierras y océanos para 2030. Los científicos advierten que algunos arrecifes más débiles podrían dejarse de lado para enfocar recursos en los más saludables. Esta investigación ofrece un punto de partida claro para una mejor conservación marina.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han identificado un mapa grande y detallado de arrecifes de coral que pueden sobrevivir al cambio climático, ofreciendo un camino claro para esfuerzos de protección dirigidos en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant underwater scene featuring a vast, flourishing coral reef teeming with diverse marine life, illuminated by gentle sunlight filtering through clear ocean water, with a detailed, softly colored map overlay subtly highlighting expansive reef areas to symbolize climate resilience and hope for marine conservation.

articles.title: UN chief: Targeting methane is a climate battle ‘we can win’
articles.summary: Amid efforts to cool global warming, the battle hinges on targeting such super-pollutants as methane, which emits one third of the world’s greenhouse gases and “is a fight we can win”, the UN chief on Wednesday.
articles.content:
“The climate crisis is accelerating, and we are now on course to overshoot the 1.5°C limit in the coming years,” UN Secretary-General António Guterres told delegates at the super-pollutants reception during London Climate Action Week, which runs from 20 to 28 June.

“Reducing methane is a fight we can win and benefit from in our own time,” he said. “Our task is to keep that overshoot as small, short and safe as possible and to bring temperatures back down. That can’t happen without drastically reducing emissions, starting now, and accelerating the shift away from fossil fuels, starting now.”

To do so demands that the world move fast on super-pollutants, which are potent greenhouse gases behind nearly half the global warming to date.

Existing tech can eliminate ‘super super-pollutant’ methane

Invisible, odourless and driving nearly a third of current global warming, methane is the key ingredient in natural gas and “is the super super-pollutant”, the UN chief said, adding that, unlike carbon dioxide, it breaks down in a decade or two.

“Cutting methane is the single fastest brake we can pull on a warming planet,” he said, pointing to the newly launched global Call to Action on Methane,

The International Energy Agency finds that around 70 per cent of methane from oil and gas can be eliminated with existing technologies at low or no net cost, and thanks to satellites, “we can track methane pollution where it happens as it happens,” he said.

‘Age of voluntary action is over’

The UN chief set out three steps governments and industry can take towards tackling methane’s negative effects:

Detect and fix every leak and eliminate routine flaring and cold venting

Make emissions measurable, reportable and verifiable

Adopt a science-based global methane standard and build a market for near-zero-methane energy

“Countries like Norway have already shown the way,” he said. “If every producer matched its standards, methane from oil and gas would fall by 90 per cent.”

The world acted to heal the ozone layer and phase out leaded petrol and now, it must act on methane pollution, the UN chief said, emphasising that “the age of voluntary action is over.”

Indeed, more than 70 per cent of the reduction potential lies within the G20 and much of it within the fossil fuel sector, and “that is where we must zero in to zero out methane.”

Tweet URL

‘Test of climate solidarity’

Developing countries need finance, technology and capacity to accelerate action across agriculture, waste and fossil fuels, the Secretary-General said.

“This is also a test of climate solidarity,” he declared, pledging that the UN will stand with every nation ready to act.

“Let’s be the generation that pulls the climate emergency brake in time.”

Financing climate action

Elaborating on that theme at a related financing for development forum in London, the UN chief outlined ways to help countries along a greener path.

The forum focused on unlocking the finance required to help countries transform and adapt to the climate crisis at a time when “climate adaptation is no longer about preparing for a distant future,” Mr. Guterres said.

“It’s about managing risks in real time as the searing heat now gripping London and far beyond makes unmistakably clear our climate is changing faster than our systems, our infrastructure and our institutions can handle,” he said.

Ensuring ‘climate justice’

With the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) confirming that the past 11 years have been the hottest on record and scientists expecting global temperatures to exceed 1.5°C in the coming years, he said “we’re entering a new era of climate risk.”

As such, adaptation is a matter of “climate justice” while also being an economic necessity and development and security imperative, he said.

“Multilateral development banks, climate funds, donors, insurers and development partners must join forces to put pre-arranged finance within reach of developing countries,” he said.

“Together, let’s ensure that resilience becomes the foundation for a safer, more secure and more sustainable future and that resources are made available for that to be possible.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and actionable global climate initiative focused on drastically reducing methane emissions, a major contributor to global warming. It highlights existing technologies, measurable goals, and international cooperation, including support for developing countries, which together represent a meaningful step toward mitigating climate change with broad societal and environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: UN chief highlights methane cut as fastest way to slow climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world is facing a climate crisis, with global temperatures rising quickly and threatening to pass a dangerous limit of 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Methane, a powerful greenhouse gas, is responsible for about one third of the warming caused by human activities.

**What happened** – At London Climate Action Week in June, UN Secretary-General António Guterres emphasized the urgent need to reduce methane emissions. He explained that methane breaks down faster in the atmosphere than carbon dioxide, so cutting it can quickly slow warming. He introduced a global Call to Action on Methane, urging governments and industries to fix leaks, measure emissions, and adopt strict methane standards.

**Impact** – This focus on methane is important because existing technology can cut about 70% of methane emissions from oil and gas at little or no cost. Countries like Norway have already shown success in reducing methane pollution. Unlike other climate actions that take decades to show results, methane cuts can produce faster benefits, making it a practical and effective way to slow climate change now.

**What's next step** – The UN calls for stronger global rules and cooperation, especially among the largest methane emitters like G20 countries and the fossil fuel sector. Developing nations will receive support with funding and technology to help reduce methane in agriculture, waste, and energy. The goal is to turn voluntary efforts into mandatory actions and build a market for cleaner energy with near-zero methane emissions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cutting methane emissions quickly using available technology offers a clear and effective path to slow global warming and protect the climate.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le chef de l’ONU souligne que réduire le méthane est le moyen le plus rapide de freiner le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le monde fait face à une crise climatique avec des températures mondiales qui augmentent rapidement, menaçant de dépasser une limite dangereuse de 1,5°C par rapport aux niveaux préindustriels. Le méthane, un puissant gaz à effet de serre, est responsable d’environ un tiers du réchauffement causé par les activités humaines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de la Semaine d’action pour le climat à Londres en juin, le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a souligné l’urgence de réduire les émissions de méthane. Il a expliqué que le méthane se décompose plus rapidement dans l’atmosphère que le dioxyde de carbone, donc le réduire peut ralentir rapidement le réchauffement. Il a lancé un appel mondial à agir contre le méthane, demandant aux gouvernements et industries de réparer les fuites, de mesurer les émissions et d’adopter des normes strictes.

**Impact** – Cette attention portée au méthane est importante car la technologie existante peut réduire environ 70 % des émissions de méthane provenant du pétrole et du gaz à faible coût ou sans coût. Des pays comme la Norvège ont déjà montré des résultats positifs. Contrairement à d’autres actions climatiques qui prennent des décennies, la réduction du méthane peut produire des bénéfices plus rapides, ce qui en fait une solution pratique et efficace dès maintenant.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU appelle à des règles mondiales plus strictes et à une coopération renforcée, notamment parmi les plus grands émetteurs comme les pays du G20 et le secteur des combustibles fossiles. Les pays en développement recevront un soutien financier et technologique pour réduire le méthane dans l’agriculture, les déchets et l’énergie. L’objectif est de transformer les efforts volontaires en actions obligatoires et de créer un marché pour une énergie presque sans méthane.

**Phrase clé** – Réduire rapidement les émissions de méthane grâce aux technologies disponibles offre une voie claire et efficace pour ralentir le réchauffement climatique et protéger le climat.
articles.personality_title_es: El jefe de la ONU destaca que reducir el metano es la forma más rápida de frenar el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El mundo enfrenta una crisis climática con temperaturas globales que aumentan rápidamente, poniendo en riesgo superar un límite peligroso de 1,5°C sobre niveles preindustriales. El metano, un gas de efecto invernadero muy potente, es responsable de alrededor de un tercio del calentamiento causado por las actividades humanas.

**Qué pasó** – En la Semana de Acción Climática en Londres en junio, el Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, destacó la urgencia de reducir las emisiones de metano. Explicó que el metano se descompone más rápido en la atmósfera que el dióxido de carbono, por lo que reducirlo puede frenar el calentamiento rápidamente. Lanzó un llamado global a la acción contra el metano, instando a gobiernos e industrias a reparar fugas, medir emisiones y adoptar normas estrictas.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque en el metano es importante porque la tecnología actual puede eliminar alrededor del 70 % de las emisiones de metano del petróleo y el gas a bajo o ningún costo. Países como Noruega ya han mostrado éxito en reducir la contaminación por metano. A diferencia de otras acciones climáticas que tardan décadas en mostrar resultados, reducir el metano puede dar beneficios más rápidos, haciendo que sea una forma práctica y efectiva de frenar el cambio climático ahora.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU pide reglas globales más fuertes y cooperación, especialmente entre los mayores emisores como los países del G20 y el sector de combustibles fósiles. Los países en desarrollo recibirán apoyo con financiamiento y tecnología para reducir el metano en agricultura, residuos y energía. El objetivo es convertir los esfuerzos voluntarios en acciones obligatorias y crear un mercado para energía con emisiones casi nulas de metano.

**Frase clave** – Reducir rápidamente las emisiones de metano con la tecnología disponible ofrece un camino claro y efectivo para frenar el calentamiento global y proteger el clima.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, glowing methane flame gently being contained and cooled by a group of interconnected hands made of flowing water and wind, set against a softly lit backdrop of a calm Earth silhouette surrounded by faint satellite orbits, using warm, natural tones to evoke hope and collective action.

articles.title: Why South Africa’s leopards shrank to half their normal size
articles.summary: A hidden population of South African leopards has revealed a remarkable evolutionary story. Researchers analyzing entire leopard genomes discovered that the Cape Floristic Region’s leopards are not only much smaller than most African leopards, but also genetically distinct after being isolated for roughly 20,000 years. Surprisingly, despite their small population, they have retained much of their genetic diversity.
articles.content:
Animals of the same species don’t always look the same. From birds with different beak shapes to mammals that vary in size or color, populations living in different places can often look very different.

What’s much harder to pin down is why these differences arise. Are they shaped by local environments? Or driven by natural or sexual selection? Or are they simply the result of the random loss of gene variants as populations become isolated and slowly diverge over time?

I’m part of a team of leopard conservationists and researchers who set out to answer some of these questions when we investigated a remarkable population of fewer than 1,000 leopards in South Africa’s Cape Floristic Region, an area that covers the country’s Western Cape, and parts of the Eastern Cape and Northern Cape.

These leopards are much smaller than leopards elsewhere on the continent – in some cases only half the body mass. For decades, researchers and conservationists have debated whether the leopards of this region are truly a separate population in terms of their genes, and if so, what might be driving that difference.

Previous genetic studies offered only limited answers. Most relied on a small number of genetic markers – specific spots in the DNA where mutations tend to happen more. This is useful in finding out large-scale patterns, but misses the finer details needed to understand how populations evolve.

To fill this gap in the research, we turned to whole-genome data. This means that instead of looking for small regions of the DNA where we expect variation, we analyzed the full sequence of paired DNA bases that make up the leopard’s genome (2.57 billion base pairs or roughly 19,000 genes in total). Together with local leopard experts and evolutionary biologists, we collected muscle or skin tissue of the leopards and compared them with genomes of leopards from other parts of Africa.

We found that leopards of the Cape are genetically different from other African leopards. This is because they’ve been isolated from other leopards for a long time and have adapted to one region. This has important implications for conservation.

Leopards in the Cape: smaller, isolated, and genetically unique

Leopards are among the most widespread large carnivores in the world, found across Africa and parts of Asia. Eight subspecies are currently recognized, including the African leopard (Panthera pardus pardus).

The African leopard found across most of sub-Saharan Africa shows extraordinary variation in coat colour, body size and skull shape. In general, leopards living in open habitats tend to be larger and paler, while those in forested areas are often smaller and darker.

The leopards of the Cape Floristic Region (a biodiverse area rich in plants found nowhere else in the world) are an exception to the pattern. They’re relatively small in mass, but until now, no one knew the reason for their distinctive appearance.

Our research found that the leopards of the Cape are not just smaller than other African leopards, they’ve also formed their own genetic group, clearly separated from leopards elsewhere in southern and eastern Africa.

A similar pattern emerged for leopards from Ghana in west Africa. In both cases, there was little evidence of recent genetic mixing with neighboring populations.

Leopards occur and move all along the length of the Cape Fold Belt mountain chain, which serves as a refuge for the cats. Beyond the northern and eastern edge of this mountain chain, it appears that leopard movement stops – the apparent barriers being very dry semi-desert in the north and high human activity in much of the Eastern Cape.

How climate change and human persecution shaped leopards in the Cape over 20,000 years

Looking back in time helped explain why this population is genetically unique. Our analyses suggest that these leopards began diverging from populations further east around 20,000-24,000 years ago, during the Last Glacial Maximum (the coldest phase of the last ice age).

We estimated this by analysing whole-genome DNA to reconstruct when populations split and how much they exchanged genes in the past. (We effectively read their shared evolutionary history, written in the genome.)

During this time, southern Africa became cooler and drier, with fewer grasslands and less food, making it harder for animals to move and survive and causing populations to become separated. More recently, leopard numbers fell sharply in the 1800s and 1900s, likely due to human hunting, habitat loss, and bounty systems that encouraged farmers to kill leopards. In 1968 the leopard bounty ended and the leopard population began to recover as conservation efforts grew.

Because they’d been isolated from other leopards and hunted, we expected our research to show that the leopards of the Cape were genetically depleted (when small populations inbreed and lose genetic diversity). Low genetic diversity makes it harder for populations to adapt to new threats like climate change, disease and human pressure. However, we found they have only slightly lower genetic diversity than other African populations – a really positive finding.

Clues in the genome point to adaptation

We also wanted to find out why the leopards of the Cape are smaller in size.

We found about 90 genes that were more common in these leopards, linked to body size, muscles, bones and energy use. These differences made sense given that the environment they live in has much smaller, more sparsely distributed prey than other leopard habitats. Leopards in the Cape feed mostly on species like rock hyrax (Procavia capensis), klipspringer (Oreotragus oreotragus) and Cape grysbok (Raphicerus melanotis).

Together, these genomic signals suggest that these leopards are small because they’ve adapted that way, and not only because of isolation or genetic drift.

Why this matters for conservation

Populations that are genetically distinct and locally adapted are often described as evolutionarily significant units. This means they represent a unique branch of a species’ evolutionary history and need specific protection so that they can continue to adapt to future change.

Leopards in the Cape Floristic Region occupy a landscape unlike any other in southern Africa, shaped by low prey availability, unique vegetation, and rapidly expanding human populations. Large fenced reserves are rare, and leopards frequently move through agricultural and urban-edge landscapes, where conflict with people is common.

To conserve these leopards, their habitats need to be connected so that they can move around unrestricted and safe from persecution. Poaching and road mortalities are two further threats that need to be addressed to ensure the persistance of leopards in the landscapes. Working in partnership with landowners and communities is essential to protect leopards.

By conserving these leopards, we are not only saving an iconic predator, but also preserving an evolutionary legacy shaped over thousands of years by one of the most distinctive landscapes on the African continent.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about a genetically distinct and locally adapted leopard population in South Africa, highlighting their evolutionary uniqueness and resilience despite isolation and human pressures. This has broad conservation implications for preserving biodiversity and evolutionary heritage in a unique ecosystem, benefiting society and the environment. The story is focused, detailed, and emphasizes positive conservation outcomes and the importance of protecting this population.
articles.personality_title: South Africa’s Cape leopards are smaller and genetically unique after 20,000 years of isolation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Leopards are found across Africa and Asia, but those living in South Africa’s Cape Floristic Region are much smaller than other African leopards. Scientists have long wondered if these smaller leopards are just a local variation or a completely separate group.

**What happened** – Researchers studied the full genomes of leopards from the Cape Floristic Region and compared them with other African leopards. They found that these Cape leopards have been isolated for about 20,000 years, making them genetically distinct. Despite their small numbers, they have kept much of their genetic diversity. The team also discovered genes linked to smaller body size, showing the leopards adapted to their environment with smaller prey.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that Cape leopards are a unique group with a long history of living separately. Their smaller size is not just random but a result of adapting to limited food sources. Knowing this helps conservationists understand how to protect these leopards better, especially since they live in an area with many human activities and fewer large protected reserves.

**What's next step** – Conservation efforts will focus on keeping habitats connected so leopards can move safely and avoid threats like hunting and road accidents. Working with local communities and landowners will be important to protect these special leopards and their environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cape leopards are a genetically unique, smaller leopard population that adapted over thousands of years and now need targeted conservation to survive in a changing landscape.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les léopards du Cap en Afrique du Sud sont plus petits et génétiquement uniques après 20 000 ans d’isolement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les léopards vivent en Afrique et en Asie, mais ceux de la région floristique du Cap en Afrique du Sud sont beaucoup plus petits que les autres léopards africains. Les scientifiques se demandaient s’il s’agissait simplement d’une variation locale ou d’un groupe complètement différent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié le génome complet des léopards du Cap et les ont comparés à ceux d’autres léopards africains. Ils ont découvert que ces léopards étaient isolés depuis environ 20 000 ans, ce qui les rend génétiquement distincts. Malgré leur petit nombre, ils ont conservé une grande partie de leur diversité génétique. L’équipe a aussi trouvé des gènes liés à leur petite taille, montrant qu’ils se sont adaptés à leur environnement avec des proies plus petites.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les léopards du Cap forment un groupe unique avec une longue histoire d’isolement. Leur petite taille n’est pas un hasard, mais une adaptation à la nourriture limitée. Cela aide les conservateurs à mieux protéger ces léopards, surtout qu’ils vivent dans une zone avec beaucoup d’activités humaines et peu de grandes réserves protégées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les efforts de conservation viseront à garder les habitats connectés pour que les léopards puissent se déplacer en sécurité et éviter les menaces comme la chasse ou les accidents de la route. Travailler avec les communautés locales et les propriétaires fonciers sera important pour protéger ces léopards particuliers et leur environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les léopards du Cap sont une population génétiquement unique et plus petite, adaptée depuis des milliers d’années, qui a besoin d’une conservation ciblée pour survivre dans un paysage en évolution.
articles.personality_title_es: Los leopardos del Cabo en Sudáfrica son más pequeños y genéticamente únicos tras 20,000 años de aislamiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los leopardos se encuentran en África y Asia, pero los que viven en la Región Florística del Cabo en Sudáfrica son mucho más pequeños que otros leopardos africanos. Los científicos se preguntaban si estos leopardos eran solo una variación local o un grupo completamente diferente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron el genoma completo de los leopardos del Cabo y los compararon con otros leopardos africanos. Descubrieron que estos leopardos han estado aislados por unos 20,000 años, lo que los hace genéticamente distintos. A pesar de su pequeño número, han mantenido gran parte de su diversidad genética. También encontraron genes relacionados con su tamaño más pequeño, mostrando que se adaptaron a su ambiente con presas más pequeñas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los leopardos del Cabo son un grupo único con una larga historia de aislamiento. Su tamaño pequeño no es casualidad, sino una adaptación a la escasez de alimento. Esto ayuda a los conservacionistas a proteger mejor a estos leopardos, especialmente porque viven en un área con muchas actividades humanas y pocas reservas grandes.

**Próximo paso** – Los esfuerzos de conservación se centrarán en mantener los hábitats conectados para que los leopardos puedan moverse con seguridad y evitar amenazas como la caza y los accidentes de tráfico. Trabajar con comunidades locales y propietarios de tierras será clave para proteger a estos leopardos especiales y su entorno.

**Frase clave** – Los leopardos del Cabo son una población genéticamente única y más pequeña que se adaptó durante miles de años y ahora necesita conservación específica para sobrevivir en un paisaje cambiante.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, sleek Cape Floristic Region leopard standing gracefully on a rocky outcrop amid the unique, richly textured fynbos vegetation, with a backdrop of the rugged Cape Fold Belt mountains under a soft golden light, symbolizing its distinct genetic heritage and adaptation to a sparse prey environment.
